Overview Quick Start Interactive Samples Root Schema example extends extensions link metadata presentation purpose rich-text status component pattern style theme token accessibility anatomy api best-practice content design-specifications guideline interaction motion principle scale state variant
What is DSDS?

DSDS (Design System Documentation Standard) is a JSON-based format for documenting design systems. It gives every piece of documentation — components, tokens, themes, styles, patterns — a predictable, machine-readable structure.

Key idea: DSDS documents the how and why of your design system — not the token values themselves. It complements the W3C Design Tokens Format which handles the what.

 What you get
  • Structured — every section has a defined shape, no guessing
  • Machine-readable — tools can parse, generate, validate, and transform it
  • Portable — not locked to any documentation tool or platform
  • Extensible — add vendor metadata without breaking interoperability
  • Validatable — JSON Schema catches errors before they reach consumers
Document Structure

A DSDS file is a JSON document with a version and one or more documentation groups. Each group contains an array of typed entities.

```json \{ "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "documentation": [ \{ "name": "My Design System", "items": [ \{ "kind": "component", "name": "button", "...": "" \}, \{ "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "...": "" \}, \{ "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "...": "" \} ] \} ] \} That's the entire top-level structure: - `dsdsVersion` — always `"0.1"` for this version - `documentation` — array of named groups, each with an `items` array - `items` — array of typed entities, mixed freely in any order **Tip:** You can organize entities into multiple groups — one for foundations, one for components, etc. — or put everything in a single group. The schema allows both. --- ## Entity Types Every entity has a `kind` discriminator that tells tools what type of thing it is. There are six kinds. | Kind | Code | Description | |------|------|-------------| | component | `"component"` | A reusable UI element — buttons, inputs, modals. Supports anatomy, API, variants, states, and design specs. | | token | `"token"` | A single design token — color, spacing, typography. Carries a resolved value, platform API mappings, and usage rules. | | token-group | `"token-group"` | A hierarchical group of related tokens. Recursive — groups can contain groups. | | theme | `"theme"` | A named set of token value overrides — dark mode, high-contrast, compact density, brand variants. | | style | `"style"` | A macro-level visual foundation — color, typography, spacing, elevation. Carries principles, scales, and motion definitions. | | pattern | `"pattern"` | A broad interaction pattern — navigation, error messaging, empty states. Describes how components compose to solve a user need. | ### Common properties Every entity shares these fields: | Property | Required | Description | |----------|----------|-------------| | `kind` | Yes | Entity type discriminator | | `name` | Yes\* | Machine-readable identifier (`^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$`, except tokens) | | `displayName` | Yes\* | Human-readable label | | `description` | Yes\* | What this entity is and does (CommonMark supported) | | `status` | No | `"stable"`, `"experimental"`, `"draft"`, `"deprecated"` — or an object with per-platform status | | `since` | No | Version when this entity was introduced | | `tags` | No | Array of freeform category tags | | `guidelines` | No | Array of typed guideline objects | \* Required for component, style, and pattern. Token requires `name` + `tokenType` + `value`. ### Status: string or object For the simple case, status is just a string: ```json ```json \{ "status": "stable" \} When you need per-platform tracking: ```json ```json \{ "status": \{ "overall": "stable", "platforms": \{ "react": \{ "status": "stable", "since": "1.0.0" \}, "ios": \{ "status": "experimental", "since": "3.0.0" \} \} \} \} --- ## The Guidelines System All structured documentation lives in the `guidelines` array on each entity. Each guideline is a typed container identified by `kind`. | Kind | Available On | Description | |------|-------------|-------------| | `anatomy` | component, pattern | Named parts that make up the entity, with optional design-token mapping per part | | `api` | component | Properties, events, slots, and CSS custom properties | | `variants` | component, pattern | Named variant axes (e.g. size, color) with discrete values | | `states` | component, pattern | Interactive states (hover, disabled, …) with per-state token overrides | | `purpose` | all | When to use / when not to use, with alternatives for negative cases | | `best-practices` | all | Actionable guidance paired with rationale and enforcement level | | `accessibility` | component, pattern | WCAG level, keyboard interactions, ARIA attributes, screen-reader behavior | | `content` | component, pattern | Writing and labelling guidelines for UI text | | `interaction` | component, pattern | Step-by-step interaction flows with optional wireframes | | `design-specifications` | component, pattern | Concrete measurements, spacing, and sizing values for implementors | | `scale` | style | Named stops along a design scale (e.g. spacing-100…spacing-900) | | `motion` | style | Named animation/transition presets with duration, easing, and purpose | | `principle` | style | Guiding design principles with rationale and examples | ### Purpose: when to use it The `purpose` guideline provides concrete scenarios. Each use case is `"positive"` (do use) or `"negative"` (don't use, with an alternative): ```json label="purpose guideline" ```json \{ "kind": "purpose", "useCases": [ \{ "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form.", "kind": "positive" \}, \{ "description": "When the action navigates to a different page.", "kind": "negative", "alternative": \{ "name": "link", "rationale": "Links carry native navigation semantics." \} \} ] \} ### Best practices: how to use it Each best practice pairs a `guidance` statement with a `rationale` and an enforcement level: ```json label="best-practices guideline" ```json \{ "kind": "best-practices", "items": [ \{ "guidance": "Limit each surface to one primary button.", "rationale": "Multiple primary buttons dilute visual hierarchy.", "kind": "required", "category": "visual-design" \} ] \} Enforcement levels: required (MUST), encouraged (SHOULD), informational (MAY, default), discouraged (SHOULD NOT), prohibited (MUST NOT). --- ## Minimal Examples These are the smallest valid entities — the floor of documentation. Copy one, fill in your content, and add guidelines as you go. ### Component A component needs `kind`, `name`, `displayName`, and `description`. Everything else is optional. ```json label="minimal component" { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "displayName": "Button", "description": "A clickable element that triggers an action.", "status": "stable", "category": "action", "guidelines": [ { "kind": "purpose", "useCases": [ { "description": "When the user needs to trigger an action such as submitting a form or confirming a dialog.", "kind": "positive" }, { "description": "When the action is navigation to another page.", "kind": "negative", "alternative": { "name": "link", "rationale": "Links communicate navigation; buttons communicate actions." } } ] } ] } Token

A token needs kind, name, tokenType, and a value with at least resolved.

{ "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } } Token Group

Groups organize tokens into hierarchies. The children array can contain tokens or nested groups.

{ "kind": "token-group", "name": "color-text", "description": "Semantic text color tokens.", "status": "stable", "tokenType": "color", "category": "semantic", "children": [ { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-primary" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-secondary" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-disabled", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#9ca3af" }, "api": { "cssCustomProperty": "--color-text-disabled" } } ] } Theme

A theme provides overrides — an array of token + value pairs that replace the default.

{ "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "displayName": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for reduced-light environments.", "status": "stable", "category": "color-mode", "overrides": [ { "token": "color-background-default", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "token": "color-text-primary", "value": { "resolved": "#f5f5f5" } }, { "token": "color-border-default", "value": { "resolved": "#333333" } } ] } Style

A style documents a visual foundation. Guidelines like principles, scale, and motion are specific to styles.

{ "kind": "style", "name": "spacing", "displayName": "Spacing", "description": "The spatial system governing layout rhythm and component density.", "status": "stable", "guidelines": [ { "kind": "principles", "items": [ { "title": "Use the scale", "description": "Always pick a value from the spacing scale. Avoid arbitrary pixel values." } ] } ] } Pattern

A pattern describes how components compose to solve a user need. It supports interactions for flow steps.

{ "kind": "pattern", "name": "form-validation", "displayName": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input on form submission and communicates errors inline.", "status": "stable", "links": [ { "kind": "component", "name": "form-field", "role": "Displays inline error messages", "required": true }, { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "role": "Triggers form submission", "required": true }, { "kind": "component", "name": "toast", "role": "Confirms successful submission" } ] } Full document {#minimal}

Here's a complete, minimal DSDS file with one of each entity type:

{ "$schema": "../schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "metadata": { "systemName": "Minimal Example" }, "documentation": [ { "name": "Minimal Design System", "items": [ { "kind": "component", "name": "button", "displayName": "Button", "description": "A clickable button for triggering actions.", "status": "stable" }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" } }, { "kind": "token-group", "name": "color-text", "children": [ { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-primary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#1a1a1a" } }, { "kind": "token", "name": "color-text-secondary", "tokenType": "color", "value": { "resolved": "#6b7280" } } ] }, { "kind": "theme", "name": "dark", "displayName": "Dark Mode", "description": "Dark color mode for low-light environments.", "status": "stable", "overrides": [ { "token": "color-text-primary", "value": { "resolved": "#f5f5f5" } }, { "token": "color-text-secondary", "value": { "resolved": "#a1a1aa" } } ] }, { "kind": "style", "name": "color", "displayName": "Color", "description": "The color system governing palette usage, semantic mapping, and accessibility.", "status": "stable" }, { "kind": "pattern", "name": "form-validation", "displayName": "Form Validation", "description": "Validates user input and communicates errors on form submission.", "status": "stable" } ] } ] }
Validate Your Document

DSDS includes a JSON Schema you can use to validate any document.

Using the bundled schema

Point your $schema at the bundled schema for editor autocompletion and inline validation:

```json \{ "$schema": "https://designsystemdocspec.org/v0.1/dsds.bundled.schema.json", "dsdsVersion": "0.1", "documentation": [] \} ### Using the CLI ```bash ```bash # Clone the repo git clone https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema.git cd design-system-documentation-schema npm install # Validate the built-in examples npm run validate # Validate your own file npx ajv validate -s spec/schema/dsds.bundled.schema.json -d my-system.dsds.json --- ## Next Steps You've seen the basics. Here's where to go deeper. | Resource | Description | |----------|-------------| | [Full Specification](index.html) | Complete schema reference for every field and constraint | | [Interactive Samples](samples.html) | Side-by-side JSON ↔ rendered documentation for real-world entities | | [JSON Schema](https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema/tree/main/spec/schema) | The raw `.schema.json` files — use for editor autocompletion | | [Example Files](https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema/tree/main/spec/examples) | Complete, validated example documents for every entity and guideline type | | [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/somerandomdude/design-system-documentation-schema/discussions) | Ask questions, share ideas, propose changes | **Getting started recipe:** 1. Copy the [minimal document](#minimal) above 2. Replace the example entities with your own design system's components and tokens 3. Add `guidelines` to each entity as your documentation matures 4. Validate with `npm run validate` to catch structural errors early

Design System Documentation Standard (DSDS) 0.1 — Draft Specification

GitHub
